summary:
The Trial of Galileo (1633 AD) was the Catholic Church's prosecution of the astronomer Galileo Galilei for advocating the Copernican heliocentric system in his Dialogue Concerning the Two Chief World Systems — a confrontation that became the defining symbol of the conflict between religious authority and scientific inquiry, though the reality was considerably more complex than the simple 'science vs. religion' narrative suggests. Galileo had been warned in 1616 not to hold or defend heliocentrism after Copernican theory was declared contrary to Scripture; his Dialogue (1632), despite nominal balance, transparently favored heliocentrism and mocked geocentric arguments, placing the geocentric arguments in the mouth of a simpleton named 'Simplicio.' Pope Urban VIII, who had been Galileo's friend and patron, reportedly felt personally humiliated by the work.

The Inquisition found Galileo 'vehemently suspect of heresy' and forced him to formally abjure heliocentrism; he was sentenced to house arrest at his villa in Arcetri, near Florence, where he spent his remaining years — during which he continued working, producing his most important mechanical work, Two New Sciences (1638). The legend that Galileo muttered 'and yet it moves' (eppur si muove) after his recantation is almost certainly apocryphal. The trial's significance lies less in what actually happened than in how it was later interpreted: Enlightenment thinkers used it to argue for the separation of scientific inquiry from religious authority, and it became a central episode in the 'conflict thesis' of science and religion — a narrative that historians of science now largely regard as oversimplified but that shaped how Western civilization understood the relationship between faith and empirical inquiry.
